joke: I told my computer I needed a break, and it said: “No problem, I’ll go to sleep.”
I bought a cactus because it was low-maintenance. So far, it’s been absolutely thorny about the whole arrangement.
I asked the moon for fashion advice, and it said, “You pull off that look every night.”
I asked the librarian if the books were any good. She said, “They’re all about the plot.”
I tried to write a joke about a broken pencil, but it was pointless.
I tried yoga once. It was a stretch.
I got a pet goldfish to help with my budget. It’s excellent at making change.
I tried to catch some fog earlier. I mist.
I told my spoon a secret, and now it’s stirring up drama.
I asked a traffic light for advice. It said, “Don’t rush me.”
I told a dinosaur a secret, but it was pre-hysterical.
I bought a mirror to boost my confidence. It’s been reflecting on my choices ever since.
I entered a joke contest at the zoo, but the judges said my punchline was too wild.
I told my shoelaces to calm down. They said they were already tied up.
I asked my stapler for advice. It said, “Hold it together.”
I tried origami once, but it just folded under pressure.
I tried to catch a squirrel, but it just went nuts.
I joined a band with my alarm clock. It always knows how to wake up the crowd.
I bought a clock with no hands. It was a real waste of time.
I asked my echo for advice. It said, “You should hear both sides.”
I went camping with a mathematician. He only brought one tent because he wanted a prime spot.
I got into an argument with a door. It was a real open-and-shut case.
I entered a staring contest with a statue. It was a stone-cold draw.
I asked an astronaut for directions, and he said, “Just follow the Milky Way.”
I told my couch a secret, and now it just sits there and supports me.
I got stuck in an elevator with a mime. It was a silent lift.
I did my laundry, and now every sock in the house is living a separate, more exciting life.
My passport and I have a lot in common: we both get stamped when we’re under pressure.
I asked the baker for advice. He said, “You need to work on your loaf-esteem.”
I told a brick wall a joke. It didn’t crack up.
